Contact Profile: Robin Young brings over 25 years of eclectic broadcast experience to her role as host of "Here and Now." Contact Profile: Alex Ashlock has been a senior producer of Morning Edition since January 1998. He produces many of the interviews host Bob Oakes does with newsmakers. Alex also reports on news and sports stories including coverage of the Marathon and the Red Sox.  Before joining the WBUR news team, Alex spent 15 years at WILL , the NPR station at the University of Illinois. He started there as a reporter, and eventually moved up to news director and then program director.  Alex has a bachelor's degree in English and a master's degree in Journalism, both from the University of Illinois.  <From Bulldog> Ashlock produces the public radio station's "Here and Now Show." "We do longer interviews that run between nine and 15 minutes, so we are looking for guests and topics with depth," he says. "The biggest thing for our show is getting interesting guests on the air. They are people who are engaging and have opinions and good stories to tell. They can speak to and comment on the news of the day. We like to get people into the studio or via satellite or ISDN line to avoid bad phone connections. If you are pitching guests related to anniversaries or big news stories that are coming up, a few weeks of notice is good. Pitches that don't work involve standard talk radio stuff and giveaways," he says.

Contact Profile: Tom Ashbrook is an award-winning journalist brought to radio by the attacks of September 11, 2001 after a distinguished career in newspaper reporting and editing.   Ashbrook was raised on an Illinois farm, attended Yale University and worked as a surveyor and dynamiter in Alaska's oil fields before turning foreign correspondent. He spent ten years in Asia, based in India, Hong Kong and Tokyo. At the Boston Globe, he directed coverage of the end of the Cold War and of the Gulf War, serving as deputy managing editor until 1996.   He was awarded the Livingston Prize for National Reporting and named a Nieman Fellow at Harvard University before taking a four-year plunge into Internet entrepreneurship, chronicled in his book The Leap. Ashbrook was enlisted to host emergency coverage of the 9/11 attacks and world response for National Public Radio through WBUR, Boston, which produces On Point.

Contact Profile: Robin Young brings over 25 years of eclectic broadcast experience to her role as host of "Here and Now." She is both a Peabody Award winning documentary filmmaker and the past host of a cooking game show. She's been a correspondent for the Discovery Channel, CBS, ABC, and for several years was substitute host and correspondent for the TODAY show on NBC. Robin may be best known to the Boston audience for her part in launching the popular Evening Magazine on WBZ-TV in the mid-70s, and for her television profiles on WNEV-TV in the mid-80's. For the past decade she's also been producing and directing documentaries.  <From Bulldog> Located at Boston University, the listeners like international, national and local news, especially about politics. Young asks that PR people send her "local stories of interest." Stories related to "immigration, war in Iraq, and the economy" pique her interest. When pitching, she advises pros to "please be brief." Email is best to reach her.

Outlet Profile: EDITORIAL PROFILE WMBR is a federally licensed FM radio station, operating at 88.1 MHz from the campus of MIT. Our signal can be heard in most of eastern Massachusetts at 88.1 FM, 365 days a year, 20-24 hours per day. WMBR is a noncommercial, all volunteer radio station. None of its members are paid for its efforts. The majority of WMBR's funding comes from listener donations with additional support from MIT.  WMBR's unique and innovative programming ranges from hip-hop to classical, punk rock to jazz, blues to celtic fiddle tunes. We offer the opportunity for MIT students and community members to learn about music, radio engineering, audio production techniques, electrical engineering, acoustics, news reporting and how to manage a small business.

Contact Profile: Alex Ashlock has been a senior producer of Morning Edition since January 1998. He produces many of the interviews host Bob Oakes does with newsmakers. Alex also reports on news and sports stories including coverage of the Marathon and the Red Sox.  Before joining the WBUR news team, Alex spent 15 years at WILL , the NPR station at the University of Illinois. He started there as a reporter, and eventually moved up to news director and then program director.  Alex has a bachelor's degree in English and a master's degree in Journalism, both from the University of Illinois.  <From Bulldog> Ashlock produces the public radio station's "Here and Now Show." "We do longer interviews that run between nine and 15 minutes, so we are looking for guests and topics with depth," he says. "The biggest thing for our show is getting interesting guests on the air. They are people who are engaging and have opinions and good stories to tell. They can speak to and comment on the news of the day. We like to get people into the studio or via satellite or ISDN line to avoid bad phone connections. If you are pitching guests related to anniversaries or big news stories that are coming up, a few weeks of notice is good. Pitches that don't work involve standard talk radio stuff and giveaways," he says.

Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Parker covers lifestyle, entertainment, sports and celebrity stories. The best PR people are friendly, believe in their product, have a willingness and ability to help promotion efforts and make their clients available to be interviewed. "When a PR person gives a more complete overview of what the guest is versed in, sometimes there is more to the interview," he says. "For example, someone just wanting to be interviewed about a book on lettuce would be boring, whereas someone who wrote a book on lettuce, but can talk about the ins and outs of gardening might be a great guest." Contact Profile: Fred Thys is the political reporter for WBUR. He reports on a wide range of political issues, with an emphasis on national security, terrorism and constitutional rights.   Thys regularly spends time at the nation's airports covering  aviation security. He recently collaborated on an "Inside Out" documentary, Unfriendly Skies. In it, he followed the struggles of a 9/11 widow and an Federal Aviation Administration special agent to uncover the security lapses that allowed hijackers to take over four planes and crash them, and documented the federal government's attempts to prevent information about 9/11 from becoming public.   In 1998, Thys won an AP award for his coverage of the changing Massachusetts eighth Congressional district, which encompasses much of Boston. In 2002, he won an AP and was a finalist for an Investigative Reporters and Editors' Award for his coverage of the FAA. In 2003, he won an Edward R. Murrow award for his coverage of aviation security and an AP for his coverage of a national security case in which the Air Force is accused of defrauding the United States Supreme Court.  Before joining WBUR in 1998, Thys was the NBC News bureau chief in Mexico City. Prior to that, he worked for CNN in a variety of positions. He served two years as a field producer in CNN's New York bureau. He also produced newscasts for CNN International.   Thys graduated from Williams College.  <From Bulldog> (Pron. "tice.") Covers politics, transportation and the state budget. Contact by phone between 9:45 a.m. and noon EST. Email anytime.

Outlet Profile: EDITORIAL PROFILE WMBR is a federally licensed FM radio station, operating at 88.1 MHz from the campus of MIT. Our signal can be heard in most of eastern Massachusetts at 88.1 FM, 365 days a year, 20-24 hours per day. WMBR is a noncommercial, all volunteer radio station. None of its members are paid for its efforts. The majority of WMBR's funding comes from listener donations with additional support from MIT.  WMBR's unique and innovative programming ranges from hip-hop to classical, punk rock to jazz, blues to celtic fiddle tunes. We offer the opportunity for MIT students and community members to learn about music, radio engineering, audio production techniques, electrical engineering, acoustics, news reporting and how to manage a small business.

Contact Profile: John Davidow joined WBUR in April 2003 after spending over two decades as a journalist in Boston. His broadcast career began in 1981 at WCVB-TV, Boston's ABC affiliate. In his seventeen years as an award winning producer at WCVB, John covered major local, national and international news. As the station's Senior Special Projects Producer John covered national presidential campaigns, investigations into international adoption in Central America, trade issues in Asia and the Exxon Valdez Oil Spill. He also covered the world of international terrorism, traveling to the Middle East several times. In 1990, John became Executive Producer of News, overseeing coverage of WCVB's major newscasts.   In 1998, John became Assistant News Director at WBZ-TV, Boston's CBS owned and operated television station. While at WBZ, John witnessed first-hand the challenges the newsroom faced after the 9/11 attacks which originated at Boston's Logan Airport. During John's tenure at WBZ, the station was recognized with the Radio and Television News Directors Association's Edward R. Murrow Award for Outstanding News Station of the Year. The award recognized the work of the WBZ newsroom in its coverage of the John F. Kennedy, Jr. plane crash and the Worcester Cold Storage fire.  John's work has been recognized with regional awards from the RTNDA, the Associated Press, and the UPI. He has also been the recipient of a number of regional Emmy Awards. John graduated cum laude from Tufts University with a Bachelor of Arts Degree in Economics.
Outlet Profile: EDITORIAL PROFILE Network Affiliation: NPR   WBUR-FM is a simulcast of WBUR-AM.

Contact Profile: Tom Ashbrook is an award-winning journalist brought to radio by the attacks of September 11, 2001 after a distinguished career in newspaper reporting and editing.   Ashbrook was raised on an Illinois farm, attended Yale University and worked as a surveyor and dynamiter in Alaska's oil fields before turning foreign correspondent. He spent ten years in Asia, based in India, Hong Kong and Tokyo. At the Boston Globe, he directed coverage of the end of the Cold War and of the Gulf War, serving as deputy managing editor until 1996.   He was awarded the Livingston Prize for National Reporting and named a Nieman Fellow at Harvard University before taking a four-year plunge into Internet entrepreneurship, chronicled in his book The Leap. Ashbrook was enlisted to host emergency coverage of the 9/11 attacks and world response for National Public Radio through WBUR, Boston, which produces On Point.

Outlet Profile: EDITORIAL PROFILE WMBR is a federally licensed FM radio station, operating at 88.1 MHz from the campus of MIT. Our signal can be heard in most of eastern Massachusetts at 88.1 FM, 365 days a year, 20-24 hours per day. WMBR is a noncommercial, all volunteer radio station. None of its members are paid for its efforts. The majority of WMBR's funding comes from listener donations with additional support from MIT.  WMBR's unique and innovative programming ranges from hip-hop to classical, punk rock to jazz, blues to celtic fiddle tunes. We offer the opportunity for MIT students and community members to learn about music, radio engineering, audio production techniques, electrical engineering, acoustics, news reporting and how to manage a small business.

Contact Profile: John Davidow joined WBUR in April 2003 after spending over two decades as a journalist in Boston. His broadcast career began in 1981 at WCVB-TV, Boston's ABC affiliate. In his seventeen years as an award winning producer at WCVB, John covered major local, national and international news. As the station's Senior Special Projects Producer John covered national presidential campaigns, investigations into international adoption in Central America, trade issues in Asia and the Exxon Valdez Oil Spill. He also covered the world of international terrorism, traveling to the Middle East several times. In 1990, John became Executive Producer of News, overseeing coverage of WCVB's major newscasts.   In 1998, John became Assistant News Director at WBZ-TV, Boston's CBS owned and operated television station. While at WBZ, John witnessed first-hand the challenges the newsroom faced after the 9/11 attacks which originated at Boston's Logan Airport. During John's tenure at WBZ, the station was recognized with the Radio and Television News Directors Association's Edward R. Murrow Award for Outstanding News Station of the Year. The award recognized the work of the WBZ newsroom in its coverage of the John F. Kennedy, Jr. plane crash and the Worcester Cold Storage fire.  John's work has been recognized with regional awards from the RTNDA, the Associated Press, and the UPI. He has also been the recipient of a number of regional Emmy Awards. John graduated cum laude from Tufts University with a Bachelor of Arts Degree in Economics.

Contact Profile: Alex Ashlock has been a senior producer of Morning Edition since January 1998. He produces many of the interviews host Bob Oakes does with newsmakers. Alex also reports on news and sports stories including coverage of the Marathon and the Red Sox.  Before joining the WBUR news team, Alex spent 15 years at WILL , the NPR station at the University of Illinois. He started there as a reporter, and eventually moved up to news director and then program director.  Alex has a bachelor's degree in English and a master's degree in Journalism, both from the University of Illinois.  <From Bulldog> Ashlock produces the public radio station's "Here and Now Show." "We do longer interviews that run between nine and 15 minutes, so we are looking for guests and topics with depth," he says. "The biggest thing for our show is getting interesting guests on the air. They are people who are engaging and have opinions and good stories to tell. They can speak to and comment on the news of the day. We like to get people into the studio or via satellite or ISDN line to avoid bad phone connections. If you are pitching guests related to anniversaries or big news stories that are coming up, a few weeks of notice is good. Pitches that don't work involve standard talk radio stuff and giveaways," he says.

Contact Profile: Fred Thys is the political reporter for WBUR. He reports on a wide range of political issues, with an emphasis on national security, terrorism and constitutional rights.   Thys regularly spends time at the nation's airports covering  aviation security. He recently collaborated on an "Inside Out" documentary, Unfriendly Skies. In it, he followed the struggles of a 9/11 widow and an Federal Aviation Administration special agent to uncover the security lapses that allowed hijackers to take over four planes and crash them, and documented the federal government's attempts to prevent information about 9/11 from becoming public.   In 1998, Thys won an AP award for his coverage of the changing Massachusetts eighth Congressional district, which encompasses much of Boston. In 2002, he won an AP and was a finalist for an Investigative Reporters and Editors' Award for his coverage of the FAA. In 2003, he won an Edward R. Murrow award for his coverage of aviation security and an AP for his coverage of a national security case in which the Air Force is accused of defrauding the United States Supreme Court.  Before joining WBUR in 1998, Thys was the NBC News bureau chief in Mexico City. Prior to that, he worked for CNN in a variety of positions. He served two years as a field producer in CNN's New York bureau. He also produced newscasts for CNN International.   Thys graduated from Williams College.  <From Bulldog> (Pron. "tice.") Covers politics, transportation and the state budget. Contact by phone between 9:45 a.m. and noon EST. Email anytime.

Outlet Profile: EDITORIAL PROFILE WMBR is a federally licensed FM radio station, operating at 88.1 MHz from the campus of MIT. Our signal can be heard in most of eastern Massachusetts at 88.1 FM, 365 days a year, 20-24 hours per day. WMBR is a noncommercial, all volunteer radio station. None of its members are paid for its efforts. The majority of WMBR's funding comes from listener donations with additional support from MIT.  WMBR's unique and innovative programming ranges from hip-hop to classical, punk rock to jazz, blues to celtic fiddle tunes. We offer the opportunity for MIT students and community members to learn about music, radio engineering, audio production techniques, electrical engineering, acoustics, news reporting and how to manage a small business.
